title: Myasthenia Gravis
oneLiner: Autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder causing fluctuating, fatigable muscle weakness — key threat is myasthenic crisis requiring urgent airway support.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure):** If the patient has reduced respiratory effort, shallow breathing, or rising pCO₂ on ABG, this is not routine weakness — call anaesthetics/senior immediately.  
- **Cholinergic crisis (overmedication with pyridostigmine):** Presents with muscle fasciculations, excessive salivation, bradycardia, and worsened weakness. The single finding that says “this is cholinergic, not myasthenic” is **fasciculations + miosis + bradycardia** (or resolved with edrophonium? rarely done in acute setting — safest imidiate action: hold pyridostigmine, get senior review).  
- **Thymoma rupture/haemorrhage:** Rare but catastrophic — acute chest pain, hypotension, superior vena cava obstruction-like picture. Any chest pain in MG → urgent CT thorax.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fatigable muscle weakness (worsens with use, improves with rest)  
  - Ptosis (unilateral or bilateral, may be intermittent)  
  - Diplopia (often the presenting symptom)  
  - Bulbar weakness: nasal voice, dysarthria, dysphagia, choking  
  - Neck flexor weakness  
  - Respiratory muscle weakness (late sign, but can present as dyspnoea or orthopnoea)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - “Ice pack test”: ptosis improves with 2 minutes of ice over eyelid (sensitive for ocular MG)  
  - “Fatigue test”: ask patient to look up for 1 minute – ptosis worsens  
  - “Peek sign”: after sustained upgaze, lid drift reveals sclera on slow closure  
  - Proximal limb weakness > distal  
  - Normal reflexes, sensation, and coordination
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS)** – poor oculobulbar involvement, depressed reflexes that potentiate after exercise, autonomic symptoms (dry mouth, impotence). **Splitter:** “Response to repetitive nerve stimulation – decrement in MG, increment in LEMS.”  
- **Stroke / brainstem TIA** – sudden onset, not fatigable, no diplopia on sustained gaze, fixed deficits.  
- **Multiple sclerosis** – episodic diplopia but with other CNS signs (optic neuritis, sensory symptoms, Uhthoff phenomenon).  
- **Thyroid eye disease** – lid lag, proptosis, no fatigability, stare, GO features.  
- **Mitochondrial myopathy (e.g., chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia)** – slowly progressive, non-fluctuating, no response to rest.  
- **Drug-induced myasthenia** (e.g., penicillamine, statins, lithium) – drug history is key.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Ice pack test  
  - Fatigability exam  
  - Speech and swallowing assessment (water swallow test if any bulbar signs)  
  - Bedside spirometry (FVC) – crucial for respiratory monitoring  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Acetylcholine receptor antibodies (AChR-Ab) – positive in ~85% generalised MG  
  - Anti-MuSK antibodies – if AChR-Ab negative  
  - Anti-voltage gated calcium channel antibodies (VGCC) – if LEMS suspected  
  - Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, TFT, glucose (exclude thyroid disease, diabetes)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT thorax (non-contrast) – mandatory to screen for thymoma  
  - MRI brain – if stroke/MS suspected (not routine for MG)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) – decrement at 2–3 Hz  
  - Single-fibre EMG – most sensitive, but not widely available  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not a single scoring system; diagnosis based on:  
  - Clinical fluctuating fatigable weakness  
  - Positive antibody test  
  - Electrophysiological confirmation if seronegative  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT thorax for thymoma** – omission risks missing a potentially resectable tumour and life-threatening complication.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If respiratory distress (FVC < 1 L or <20 ml/kg, rising pCO₂, accessory muscle use) → call anaesthetics/ICU for urgent NIPPV or intubation. **Do not wait for ABG to deteriorate.**  
  - Stop all non-essential drugs (especially those that worsen MG – see Drugs & Doses).  
  - If suspected myasthenic crisis and no contraindication: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days OR plasma exchange (PLEX) – discuss with neurology.  
  - Cholinergic crisis: withhold pyridostigmine, give atropine 0.6 mg IV if severe muscarinic symptoms (bradycardia, excess secretions).  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Neurology consult.  
  - Baseline spirometry (FVC) – monitor every 1–2 hours initially.  
  - Swallow assessment – NBM until judged safe (SLP review).  
  - Pyridostigmine – stabilise oral dose (e.g., 30–60 mg PO qds, titrate up).  
  - If poor response, start prednisolone (e.g., 10–20 mg PO daily, slow up-titration – can transiently worsen weakness, so usually do in-patient).  
  - If thymoma identified → surgical referral for thymectomy (once stable).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Steroid-sparing immunosuppressants: azathioprine (1–2 mg/kg/day – check TPMT before starting), mycophenolate mofetil, or ciclosporin.  
  - Rituximab for refractory MuSK-positive MG.  
  - Thymectomy for all patients with thymoma; also for non-thymomatous generalised MG (some guidelines).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** normal saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance (if swallowing unsafe, hold oral).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH if not on high-dose steroids (risk of GI bleed? consider PPI cover if steroids).  
  - **Diet:** NBM until speech and language therapy (SLT) assessment for dysphagia.  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 with added FVC monitoring (if FVC < 1.5 L → escalate to physio/ICU).  
  - **Caution O₂:** give only to keep SpO₂ > 94% – avoid high FiO₂ causing suppression of hypoxic drive (rare but possible).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Pyridostigmine (first-line symptomatic):** Commence 30–60 mg PO qds (taken 30–60 min before meals). Max up to 120 mg 5 times daily – **[check local policy / BNF]**.  
  - Key interactions: aminoglycosides, β-blockers, calcium channel blockers, ciprofloxacin, tetracyclines, procainamide, penicillamine, statins – all can worsen weakness.  
  - Most dangerous error: starting pyridostigmine simultaneously with high-dose prednisolone in crisis – risk of cholinergic crisis or transient steroid-induced worsening. Always start at low pred dose and titrate slowly.  
- **Prednisolone (immunosuppressive):** Initial low dose (e.g., 10–20 mg/day PO) with slow increase (every 5–7 days) to reduce risk of early deterioration. Target ~30–40 mg/day. **[Check local policy for tapering schedule.]**  
- **Azathioprine:** 1 mg/kg/day PO initially, increase to 2 mg/kg/day if tolerated. Requires TPMT genotype before starting. Monitor LFTs and FBC.  
- **IVIG / plasma exchange:** IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days or PLEX 1 session every other day (total 5–7 sessions) – neuro consult for plan.  
- **Atropine** for cholinergic crisis: 0.6 mg IV, repeat if needed (max 2 mg).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) – most feared  
  - Cholinergic crisis (pyridostigmine overdose) – bradycardia, fasciculations, worsened weakness  
  - Aspiration pneumonia from bulbar weakness  
  - Steroid-induced myopathy if high-dose steroids prolonged  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Thymoma growth or recurrence (requires CT surveillance)  
  - Osteoporosis with long-term steroids  
  - Opportunistic infections due to immunosuppression
sections.Prognosis: - Myasthenia gravis is a chronic, manageable condition. ~10–15% have a myasthenic crisis at some point. With treatment, most patients achieve good symptom control. Thymectomy in thymoma-positive patients improves outcomes. Mortality in crisis <5% with modern ICU care.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO perform the “ice pack test” on any patient with ptosis** – it’s bedside, cheap, and can confirm ocular MG quickly.  
- **DO obtain a baseline FVC on every MG admission** – it is the single best objective measure of respiratory reserve.  
- **DO start PPI if prescribing steroids** – prevent gastroduodenal bleeding.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT give high-dose steroids without in-patient monitoring** – can precipitate acute worsening within days.  
- **DO NOT prescribe drugs that block neuromuscular transmission** (e.g., gentamicin, clindamycin, beta-blockers, verapamil, lithium, penicillamine) – check BNF for full list.  
- **DO NOT attribute worsening solely to “myasthenia”** – always rule out cholinergic crisis, infection (urinary/chest), and drug-induced exacerbation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening ptosis / double vision / dysphagia / nasal regurgitation  
  - Any shortness of breath or difficulty speaking  
  - Unexplained severe weakness, especially after taking extra pyridostigmine (suspect cholinergic crisis)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Neurology outpatient clinic within 2–4 weeks  
  - Arrange FVC monitoring from home if possible (e.g., weekly with GP)  
  - Stop or adjust medications only on neurology advice
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or rising track  
  - FVC < 1.0 L or <20 ml/kg  
  - SpO₂ < 92% on room air or on low-flow O₂  
  - Rising pCO₂ on ABG (> 6.0 kPa or rising trend)  
  - Inability to swallow saliva  
  - Any sign of cholinergic crisis (fasciculations, bradycardia, miosis) not resolving with atropine  
- **Crash team criteria:**  
  - Respiratory arrest / shallow breathing  
  - Unresponsive patient  
  - Severe bradycardia

rawMarkdown:
## Myasthenia Gravis
**One-liner:** Autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder causing fluctuating, fatigable muscle weakness — key threat is myasthenic crisis requiring urgent airway support.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure):** If the patient has reduced respiratory effort, shallow breathing, or rising pCO₂ on ABG, this is not routine weakness — call anaesthetics/senior immediately.  
- **Cholinergic crisis (overmedication with pyridostigmine):** Presents with muscle fasciculations, excessive salivation, bradycardia, and worsened weakness. The single finding that says “this is cholinergic, not myasthenic” is **fasciculations + miosis + bradycardia** (or resolved with edrophonium? rarely done in acute setting — safest imidiate action: hold pyridostigmine, get senior review).  
- **Thymoma rupture/haemorrhage:** Rare but catastrophic — acute chest pain, hypotension, superior vena cava obstruction-like picture. Any chest pain in MG → urgent CT thorax.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fatigable muscle weakness (worsens with use, improves with rest)  
  - Ptosis (unilateral or bilateral, may be intermittent)  
  - Diplopia (often the presenting symptom)  
  - Bulbar weakness: nasal voice, dysarthria, dysphagia, choking  
  - Neck flexor weakness  
  - Respiratory muscle weakness (late sign, but can present as dyspnoea or orthopnoea)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - “Ice pack test”: ptosis improves with 2 minutes of ice over eyelid (sensitive for ocular MG)  
  - “Fatigue test”: ask patient to look up for 1 minute – ptosis worsens  
  - “Peek sign”: after sustained upgaze, lid drift reveals sclera on slow closure  
  - Proximal limb weakness > distal  
  - Normal reflexes, sensation, and coordination  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS)** – poor oculobulbar involvement, depressed reflexes that potentiate after exercise, autonomic symptoms (dry mouth, impotence). **Splitter:** “Response to repetitive nerve stimulation – decrement in MG, increment in LEMS.”  
- **Stroke / brainstem TIA** – sudden onset, not fatigable, no diplopia on sustained gaze, fixed deficits.  
- **Multiple sclerosis** – episodic diplopia but with other CNS signs (optic neuritis, sensory symptoms, Uhthoff phenomenon).  
- **Thyroid eye disease** – lid lag, proptosis, no fatigability, stare, GO features.  
- **Mitochondrial myopathy (e.g., chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia)** – slowly progressive, non-fluctuating, no response to rest.  
- **Drug-induced myasthenia** (e.g., penicillamine, statins, lithium) – drug history is key.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Ice pack test  
  - Fatigability exam  
  - Speech and swallowing assessment (water swallow test if any bulbar signs)  
  - Bedside spirometry (FVC) – crucial for respiratory monitoring  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Acetylcholine receptor antibodies (AChR-Ab) – positive in ~85% generalised MG  
  - Anti-MuSK antibodies – if AChR-Ab negative  
  - Anti-voltage gated calcium channel antibodies (VGCC) – if LEMS suspected  
  - Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, TFT, glucose (exclude thyroid disease, diabetes)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT thorax (non-contrast) – mandatory to screen for thymoma  
  - MRI brain – if stroke/MS suspected (not routine for MG)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) – decrement at 2–3 Hz  
  - Single-fibre EMG – most sensitive, but not widely available  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not a single scoring system; diagnosis based on:  
  - Clinical fluctuating fatigable weakness  
  - Positive antibody test  
  - Electrophysiological confirmation if seronegative  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT thorax for thymoma** – omission risks missing a potentially resectable tumour and life-threatening complication.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If respiratory distress (FVC < 1 L or <20 ml/kg, rising pCO₂, accessory muscle use) → call anaesthetics/ICU for urgent NIPPV or intubation. **Do not wait for ABG to deteriorate.**  
  - Stop all non-essential drugs (especially those that worsen MG – see Drugs & Doses).  
  - If suspected myasthenic crisis and no contraindication: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days OR plasma exchange (PLEX) – discuss with neurology.  
  - Cholinergic crisis: withhold pyridostigmine, give atropine 0.6 mg IV if severe muscarinic symptoms (bradycardia, excess secretions).  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Neurology consult.  
  - Baseline spirometry (FVC) – monitor every 1–2 hours initially.  
  - Swallow assessment – NBM until judged safe (SLP review).  
  - Pyridostigmine – stabilise oral dose (e.g., 30–60 mg PO qds, titrate up).  
  - If poor response, start prednisolone (e.g., 10–20 mg PO daily, slow up-titration – can transiently worsen weakness, so usually do in-patient).  
  - If thymoma identified → surgical referral for thymectomy (once stable).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Steroid-sparing immunosuppressants: azathioprine (1–2 mg/kg/day – check TPMT before starting), mycophenolate mofetil, or ciclosporin.  
  - Rituximab for refractory MuSK-positive MG.  
  - Thymectomy for all patients with thymoma; also for non-thymomatous generalised MG (some guidelines).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** normal saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance (if swallowing unsafe, hold oral).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH if not on high-dose steroids (risk of GI bleed? consider PPI cover if steroids).  
  - **Diet:** NBM until speech and language therapy (SLT) assessment for dysphagia.  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 with added FVC monitoring (if FVC < 1.5 L → escalate to physio/ICU).  
  - **Caution O₂:** give only to keep SpO₂ > 94% – avoid high FiO₂ causing suppression of hypoxic drive (rare but possible).  

### Drugs & Doses
- **Pyridostigmine (first-line symptomatic):** Commence 30–60 mg PO qds (taken 30–60 min before meals). Max up to 120 mg 5 times daily – **[check local policy / BNF]**.  
  - Key interactions: aminoglycosides, β-blockers, calcium channel blockers, ciprofloxacin, tetracyclines, procainamide, penicillamine, statins – all can worsen weakness.  
  - Most dangerous error: starting pyridostigmine simultaneously with high-dose prednisolone in crisis – risk of cholinergic crisis or transient steroid-induced worsening. Always start at low pred dose and titrate slowly.  
- **Prednisolone (immunosuppressive):** Initial low dose (e.g., 10–20 mg/day PO) with slow increase (every 5–7 days) to reduce risk of early deterioration. Target ~30–40 mg/day. **[Check local policy for tapering schedule.]**  
- **Azathioprine:** 1 mg/kg/day PO initially, increase to 2 mg/kg/day if tolerated. Requires TPMT genotype before starting. Monitor LFTs and FBC.  
- **IVIG / plasma exchange:** IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days or PLEX 1 session every other day (total 5–7 sessions) – neuro consult for plan.  
- **Atropine** for cholinergic crisis: 0.6 mg IV, repeat if needed (max 2 mg).  

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) – most feared  
  - Cholinergic crisis (pyridostigmine overdose) – bradycardia, fasciculations, worsened weakness  
  - Aspiration pneumonia from bulbar weakness  
  - Steroid-induced myopathy if high-dose steroids prolonged  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Thymoma growth or recurrence (requires CT surveillance)  
  - Osteoporosis with long-term steroids  
  - Opportunistic infections due to immunosuppression  

### Prognosis
- Myasthenia gravis is a chronic, manageable condition. ~10–15% have a myasthenic crisis at some point. With treatment, most patients achieve good symptom control. Thymectomy in thymoma-positive patients improves outcomes. Mortality in crisis <5% with modern ICU care.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO perform the “ice pack test” on any patient with ptosis** – it’s bedside, cheap, and can confirm ocular MG quickly.  
- **DO obtain a baseline FVC on every MG admission** – it is the single best objective measure of respiratory reserve.  
- **DO start PPI if prescribing steroids** – prevent gastroduodenal bleeding.  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DO NOT give high-dose steroids without in-patient monitoring** – can precipitate acute worsening within days.  
- **DO NOT prescribe drugs that block neuromuscular transmission** (e.g., gentamicin, clindamycin, beta-blockers, verapamil, lithium, penicillamine) – check BNF for full list.  
- **DO NOT attribute worsening solely to “myasthenia”** – always rule out cholinergic crisis, infection (urinary/chest), and drug-induced exacerbation.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening ptosis / double vision / dysphagia / nasal regurgitation  
  - Any shortness of breath or difficulty speaking  
  - Unexplained severe weakness, especially after taking extra pyridostigmine (suspect cholinergic crisis)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Neurology outpatient clinic within 2–4 weeks  
  - Arrange FVC monitoring from home if possible (e.g., weekly with GP)  
  - Stop or adjust medications only on neurology advice  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or rising track  
  - FVC < 1.0 L or <20 ml/kg  
  - SpO₂ < 92% on room air or on low-flow O₂  
  - Rising pCO₂ on ABG (> 6.0 kPa or rising trend)  
  - Inability to swallow saliva  
  - Any sign of cholinergic crisis (fasciculations, bradycardia, miosis) not resolving with atropine  
- **Crash team criteria:**  
  - Respiratory arrest / shallow breathing  
  - Unresponsive patient  
  - Severe bradycardia
